summary: Cleaned up GitHub Actions regression-build output for objdiff-based matching checks. The workflow now suppresses noisy Rust logs with RUST_LOG=error, runs `ninja diff` only when `ninja` fails in link mode, and changes the jq reporting logic so regression failures are summarized by unit name with affected function names instead of dumping every function's fuzzy-match percentages.
what_changed: Added `export RUST_LOG=error` in the GitHub Actions Build step to reduce noisy Rust-originated logging during CI.
Changed link-mode execution from unconditional `ninja` followed by `ninja diff` to `ninja || ninja diff`, so the expensive/noisy diff target is only run after a build failure.
Reworked the jq expression used after `objdiff-cli report changes` to filter for units containing functions that regressed from `fuzzy_match_percent` 100 to a lower value.
Changed regression output from a flat list of `{name, from, to}` records for all functions to a per-unit object mapping the unit name to affected function names.
Changed status computation to slurp the filtered jq results and return success when the result list is empty, failure otherwise.
smart_moves: Reduced CI log volume without weakening the regression gate: the workflow still fails when any previously 100% matched function drops below 100%.
Made failure output more actionable by grouping broken functions under their containing unit instead of printing every function's fuzzy-match data.
Used jq filtering before status calculation, so the same filtered result drives both human-readable output and pass/fail behavior.
Avoided running `ninja diff` on successful link builds, preserving diff detail for failure cases while keeping normal CI output cleaner.
decomp_lessons: For matching-regression CI, report only regressions that matter to current progress, such as functions dropping from 100% match to a lower fuzzy match, rather than all measured functions.
A compact per-unit list of broken function names is often more useful to decomp contributors than raw fuzzy-match percentage dumps.
CI should distinguish between normal successful build output and diagnostic output; expensive or verbose diff commands can be gated behind failures.
Workflow changes can materially improve decomp velocity even when no C source or assembly matching code changes.
naming_conventions: The workflow uses `mode`, `config_args`, `expected`, `actual`, `changes`, `results`, and `status` shell variables for build and report state.
The objdiff JSON fields referenced are `.units[]`, `.functions[]`, `.name`, `.from.fuzzy_match_percent`, and `.to.fuzzy_match_percent`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used objdiff fuzzy-match percentages as the regression signal.
Specifically treated functions with `.from.fuzzy_match_percent == 100` and `.to.fuzzy_match_percent < .from` as broken matching regressions.
Generated expected and actual objdiff reports, compared them with `objdiff-cli report changes`, then post-processed the changes JSON with jq.
follow_up_queries: Find later workflow changes that adjust objdiff-cli regression reporting or fuzzy_match_percent thresholds.
Search for CI failures around `One or more functions were broken!` to see how this output was consumed.
Check whether subsequent PRs refined the jq report shape for matching regressions.
